description:
secretx-systemd/src/lib.rs:110 reads the credential via:

    tokio::fs::read(&path).await

The sibling secretx-file crate uses tokio::task::spawn_blocking + std::fs::read instead (secretx-file/src/lib.rs:77-93). Both target regular files in the local filesystem; both are async; the implementation choice differs.

Engineering basis: tokio::fs internally schedules blocking work onto the runtime's blocking thread pool already (using BLOCKING_TASK_LIMIT-bounded spawn_blocking), so functionally the two patterns are similar. The difference shows up under heavy load: tokio::fs is convenience but tokio's docs note the operations 'are not zero-cost abstractions' and counsel using spawn_blocking explicitly when the pool sizing matters.

For this backend the file in question is a tmpfs read of a few hundred bytes that has been pre-decrypted by systemd at exec — there is no syscall blocking that the choice would meaningfully affect. So the inconsistency is cosmetic, not a bug. But it's worth deciding workspace-wide which idiom this family uses, and aligning all backends to it. Right now systemd is the outlier (file uses spawn_blocking; desktop uses spawn_blocking; only systemd uses tokio::fs::read).

Recommendation: switch to spawn_blocking + std::fs::read to match the family pattern, OR document why tokio::fs::read is preferred here and propagate to the others.
title: tokio::fs::read in get() vs spawn_blocking + std::fs in sibling secretx-file
